content.claim: Cross-national meta-analytic evidence (2000-2021 studies) shows tracking and academic segregation are associated with negative effects on inequality of academic performance outcomes, with no significant measured efficiency losses from de-tracking; countries improving equity by increasing school heterogeneity also showed higher student competency progress.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Demography lens
limitations: Meta-analytic finding is from international literature, not Hungary-specific causal test
Generalizability to Hungarian institutional context (high selection intensity, dormitory-dependent access) unclear
No source documents outcome-specific effect sizes (e.g., mathematics vs. reading, or timing of observed effects)
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
